questions.question: A single `new int` on the hot path of a latency-critical system can cause a P99 spike of 10+ microseconds. What is the primary mechanism for this spike?
options: The new expression itself compiles to hundreds of instructions
Heap allocation may trigger a page fault or contend on the allocator's internal lock
The int must be zero-initialized which is slow on most CPUs
new always triggers a system call to mmap a new page
questions.explanation: Heap allocation is non-deterministic: it may hit a free-list quickly (fast path) or trigger a page fault, coalesce free blocks, or contend on an internal mutex (slow path). This bimodal distribution is exactly what blows the P99 tail.

questions.question: In a lock-free SPSC ring buffer, the producer stores data before incrementing write_pos with `memory_order_release`, and the consumer loads write_pos with `memory_order_acquire`. What guarantee does this provide?
options: All threads see the write in the same order
The consumer sees all data written before the producer's release store to write_pos
No reordering of any kind is possible
The buffer will never overflow
questions.explanation: release-acquire forms a synchronization pair: the consumer's acquire load on write_pos guarantees it sees all writes that happened before the producer's release store on write_pos. This is cheaper than seq_cst because it doesn't require a global total order.

questions.question: You have two atomic counters incremented by different threads. P99 latency is terrible despite using `memory_order_relaxed`. Adding `alignas(64)` to each counter fixes it. Why?
options: alignas(64) makes the atomic operations faster on x86
The two counters were on the same 64-byte cache line, causing false sharing; padding puts them on separate lines
aligned memory is required for relaxed atomics to work correctly
The compiler generates better code for aligned atomics
questions.explanation: Without padding, both counters share a cache line. Each increment invalidates the line in the other core's cache, causing ping-pong traffic (false sharing). alignas(64) forces each counter onto its own cache line, eliminating the invalidation traffic.

questions.question: CRTP eliminates virtual dispatch overhead. What is the trade-off?
options: CRTP requires runtime type information (RTTI)
Each derived type instantiation creates a separate Base template instantiation, increasing code size (binary bloat)
CRTP can only be used with a single derived class
CRTP makes the code slower due to template recursion
questions.explanation: CRTP generates a separate copy of Base<Derived> for each Derived type. This allows inlining and static dispatch but bloats the binary. In tight latency loops the I-cache savings from inlining usually outweigh code-size costs, but for rarely-used types with large base implementations, the trade-off may not be worth it.

questions.question: A seqlock reader sees an odd sequence number, then yields and retries. Why does this guarantee a consistent read without any locks?
options: Odd sequence numbers are impossible so the reader never actually retries
An odd sequence means a write is in progress; the reader waits until the writer finishes (even sequence) and then re-reads, verifying the sequence number hasn't changed between two reads
The seqlock uses a mutex internally that the reader acquires
Memory ordering prevents the reader from seeing partial writes
questions.explanation: The writer increments seq (odd), writes data, then increments seq (even). A reader captures seq before and after reading data. If seq changed (or was odd), the data may be inconsistent so the reader retries. This is lock-free for readers — they never block, only spin-retry.

questions.question: Why does replacing `pos % size` with `pos & (size - 1)` in a ring buffer matter for low-latency code?
options: It doesn't matter; compilers optimize both identically
Bitwise AND is a single-cycle instruction on most CPUs while modulo requires division, which can take 20-40 cycles and is variable-latency
The modulo can overflow for large values of pos
The bitwise AND prevents integer promotion bugs
questions.explanation: Division/modulo is one of the slowest integer operations (20-40 cycles on x86, variable latency). Bitwise AND is 1 cycle and pipelined. For a power-of-2 ring buffer, this substitution eliminates a latency-variable operation from the hot path. The size must be a power of 2 for the equivalence to hold.
